title: 7 Monetization Strategies for Indie Hackers in 2025
description: From SaaS subscriptions to affiliate revenue, here are the most effective ways to monetize a developer tool or content site as a solo founder.
author: MyAppAI Team
body:
Building something people want is hard. Getting paid for it is a different skill entirely. Here are the seven monetization strategies that actually work for indie hackers and solo developers in 2025.

## 1. SaaS Subscriptions

The gold standard for recurring revenue. If your tool saves people time or money on a recurring basis, a monthly subscription is the most natural fit.

**What works:** Tiered pricing (free/pro/team), annual discounts, usage-based billing for API-heavy products.

**What doesn't:** Charging too early before you have product-market fit. Get 10 paying customers before optimizing pricing.

**Tools:** Stripe for payments, Lemon Squeezy for simpler tax handling, Paddle for international.

## 2. Affiliate Revenue

Recommend tools you genuinely use and earn a commission when people sign up. This works especially well for developer tools, hosting, and SaaS products.

**What works:** Writing honest, detailed comparisons. Readers trust specificity — "I use Cloudflare Pages for all my static sites because X" converts better than generic recommendations.

**Commission rates:** Hosting (20-50% recurring), SaaS tools (20-30% first year), courses (30-50% one-time).

**Best programs:** Cloudflare, DigitalOcean, Vercel, Notion, Airtable, ConvertKit.

## 3. Google AdSense

Passive income from display ads. Works best for content sites with 10,000+ monthly visitors. Revenue is low per visitor ($1-5 RPM typically) but scales with traffic.

**What works:** High-traffic tutorial content, comparison posts, and tool directories. SEO is the moat.

**What doesn't:** Trying to monetize a site with under 1,000 monthly visitors — the math doesn't work.

**Getting approved:** You need original content, an About page, a Contact page, a Privacy Policy, and no policy violations. Quality over quantity.

## 4. Digital Products

One-time purchases with no ongoing support burden. Templates, starter kits, UI component libraries, and prompt packs all sell well in developer communities.

**What works:** Solving a specific, painful problem. A $49 Next.js SaaS starter kit that saves 20 hours of setup is an easy sell.

**Platforms:** Gumroad, Lemon Squeezy, your own Stripe checkout.

## 5. Sponsored Content

Once you have an audience, companies will pay to reach them. Newsletter sponsorships, sponsored blog posts, and YouTube integrations all work.

**Rates:** $50-500 per newsletter issue (1,000-10,000 subscribers), $500-5,000 per sponsored post (depending on traffic).

**What works:** Only sponsor products you'd genuinely recommend. Your audience can tell the difference.

## 6. Consulting and Services

Your open-source projects and blog posts are a portfolio. Companies will pay for your expertise.

**What works:** Positioning yourself as the expert in a specific niche. "I help SaaS companies integrate AI features" is more compelling than "I'm a full-stack developer."

**Rates:** $100-300/hour for senior developers, $5,000-20,000 for project-based work.

## 7. Open Core / Freemium

Release the core product open-source, charge for advanced features, hosting, or support. This is how tools like Supabase, Plausible, and Cal.com operate.

**What works:** When the open-source version is genuinely useful and the paid version solves a real pain point (usually around scale, support, or convenience).

**What doesn't:** Crippling the free version so much that it's not actually useful — this kills trust.

## The Real Answer

Most successful indie hackers combine 2-3 of these. A typical stack: SaaS subscriptions as the primary revenue, affiliate links on the blog, and occasional consulting. Start with one, validate it, then layer in others.

The biggest mistake is trying to monetize too early. Build something 100 people love before worrying about revenue.
